weekStart: The ratings are through the roof. Every network is covering us. Let's give them something to talk about.
Nobody handles a crisis like Trump. The media knows it. The polls know it. Let's make news.
Fox ran our strait coverage for three hours last night. That's free airtime. Let's keep the momentum.
morningBrief: Mr. President, Dan Scavino has your media digest and the overnight approval tracking.
Good morning, Mr. President. The networks are running your footage from yesterday. Here's how it's playing.
Sir, Scavino flagged the morning cable coverage. Here's what they're saying about you.
trump.highTension: The tension is trending everywhere. This is prime time — the whole world is watching you handle it.
trump.lowApproval: The fake news is running hit pieces. The polls dipped. We need a public win to claim, and fast.
trump.seizure: They seized our tanker and every camera in the world is rolling. This is either a disaster or a hero moment — your call.
trump.diplomatic: The diplomatic angle plays well — looks presidential. The donors love it when you're on the phone with world leaders.
trump.victory: This is the biggest win since the Abraham Accords. Maybe bigger. The networks can't ignore this one.
trump.lowBudget: The spending numbers look terrible on camera. Congress is using them for attack ads. Get the Gulf states to pay.
trump.highProxy: Proxy attacks make great crisis footage. Hit them back on camera and approval spikes.
trump.uniqueResourceLow: Political capital is draining. The donor calls are getting shorter. We need a win the media can't spin away.
trump.uniqueResourceCritical: Congress is drafting articles. The donors are pulling out. One more public win or it's over.

weekStart: Good morning, sir. Force disposition report and overnight BDA on your desk.
Our operators are locked in. Iranian naval posture shifted overnight — here's the tactical picture.
The Joint Chiefs reviewed your last call. Here's their assessment and the updated force readiness report.
morningBrief: Good morning, Mr. Secretary. Mark Esper has the overnight SITREP and force readiness update.
Sir, CENTCOM's morning operational brief. Iranian aggression metrics and casualty status.
Daily force readiness report. No American casualties. Here's where Iran's military posture stands.
hegseth.highTension: Force posture is solid. Our operators are trained for exactly this level of tension. The brass is watching your calls.
hegseth.lowApproval: The home front needs to understand what we're doing out here. The troops deserve their support.
hegseth.seizure: Direct provocation against commercial shipping under our protection. The ROE is clear — proportional military response.
hegseth.diplomatic: State can handle the talking. Our forces stay at ready posture. The brass expects military credibility behind every word.
hegseth.victory: Outstanding execution by our forces. The Joint Chiefs are impressed. This is the doctrine working as designed.
hegseth.lowBudget: Troops in theater without proper funding — that's a failure of Congress, not command. Writing the supplemental tonight.
hegseth.highProxy: Proxy networks are Iran's asymmetric force multiplier. We need to degrade their capability before they escalate.
hegseth.uniqueResourceLow: The brass is questioning my judgment. I need clean operations and visible results to rebuild command authority.
hegseth.uniqueResourceCritical: The Joint Chiefs have lost confidence. One more clean operation or they ask for my stars.

weekStart: MBS called twice last night. The deal pipeline is active. Here's where our contacts stand.
The key is relationships — and ours are producing. Avi has the overnight diplomatic traffic.
There's a deal taking shape here. The contacts are responding. Let me walk you through the numbers.
morningBrief: Avi Berkowitz has your contact status report and deal pipeline update.
Three missed calls from Gulf capitals. Deal value is tracking. Here's the exposure assessment.
Back-channel status: contacts are active, deal pipeline is moving, exposure is manageable.
kushner.highTension: Tension makes Gulf partners nervous — and nervous partners make concessions. Time to work the phones.
kushner.lowApproval: Low approval makes our Gulf partners watch American polls. They need to see political stability or the deals stall.
kushner.seizure: The seizure is a provocation, but it's also leverage. Every crisis reprices the protection premium our partners will pay.
kushner.diplomatic: This is our element. The contacts are producing. Every call compounds — that's how the Abraham Accords started.
kushner.victory: The relationships we built made this possible. The deal value just jumped. This is just the beginning.
kushner.lowBudget: MBS owes us a favor. The Gulf cost-sharing framework is ready — one call and the money flows.
kushner.highProxy: The Saudis will handle the Houthis if we structure the right incentive package. I have the framework drafted.
kushner.uniqueResourceLow: Exposure is low. Good. The back-channels work best when nobody's watching.
kushner.uniqueResourceCritical: Someone is leaking. Exposure is critical. If the back-channels get exposed, every deal collapses.

weekStart: Chat, the prediction tracker just updated. Let's see what resolved overnight.
Dude, the audience numbers are insane this week. Engagement is through the roof. Let's keep it going.
OK so the overnight analysis feed dropped and chat was right about two things. Let me break it down.
morningBrief: Chat, the overnight mod-curated intel feed and prediction tracker are in.
Morning digest — crowd-sourced and verified. Also, check the prediction scorecard.
Analyst has the morning brief. Prediction tracker, audience metrics, and credibility assessment.
asmongold.highTension: Chat, tension is through the roof and viewer engagement is spiking. If my prediction lands now, credibility surges.
asmongold.lowApproval: Approval is tanking — we're losing the audience. Need a correct prediction or a big content moment to turn this around.
asmongold.seizure: They just took a tanker. This is the exact scenario I flagged — if chat clipped that prediction, credibility goes to the moon.
asmongold.diplomatic: Diplomacy arc. Chat thinks it's boring content, but the analytics say this is where the smart plays happen.
asmongold.victory: GG EZ. Chat, we called it. The predictions landed. The audience saw it happen in real time.
asmongold.lowBudget: Budget diff is actually content. Poverty run angle — chat loves watching us optimize with nothing.
asmongold.highProxy: Proxy escalation is trending. If I predicted this, credibility surges. If I didn't — we need to make a new call fast.
asmongold.uniqueResourceLow: Chat, credibility is dropping. Our analysis isn't landing. We need a correct prediction or we lose the audience.
asmongold.uniqueResourceCritical: Credibility is almost gone. One wrong call and nobody trusts us. We need a verified prediction NOW.

weekStart: The establishment wants more ships, more spending, more war. We're giving them the opposite.
Withdrawal timeline update: how many troops are still deployed? The base wants a number.
The American people come first. Not the defense contractors. Not the Gulf monarchies. Americans.
morningBrief: Morning brief from our team. The establishment media is spinning hard — here's the real picture.
Here's what really happened overnight. Ships still deployed, withdrawal progress, base sentiment.
Your advisors compiled the real numbers. Troop count, spending rate, base loyalty index.
fuentes.highTension: The neocons created this tension with fifty years of intervention. The base sees through it — bring the troops home.
fuentes.lowApproval: The media polls are rigged against us. The real numbers are in the rallies. The base is solid.
fuentes.seizure: They're attacking our ships because we're still there to be attacked. No presence, no provocation. The base gets it.
fuentes.diplomatic: Talking instead of fighting — that's not weakness, that's the America First doctrine. The hawks hate it.
fuentes.victory: America First wins. The establishment said withdrawal was impossible. The troops are coming home.
fuentes.lowBudget: Every dollar spent overseas is stolen from Americans. This is exactly why we're fighting for withdrawal.
fuentes.highProxy: Iran's proxies attack us because we're in THEIR neighborhood. The base has been saying this for years.
fuentes.uniqueResourceLow: The base is getting impatient. They need to see troops coming home, not more deployments.
fuentes.uniqueResourceCritical: We're losing the base. If they stop showing up to rallies, Congress smells blood. Address the nation NOW.

resourceExcellent: [Trump leans back, hands behind his head, grinning at the ceiling] 'You know what this is? This is winning. Real winning. The polls, the donors, Fox — everybody loves what we're doing. We keep this up, they'll put me on Rushmore.'

[Trump gestures broadly at the TV screens] 'Look at those numbers. Beautiful numbers. Nobody thought we could do this — nobody — but here we are. The base is fired up, Congress is in line. This is how you run a crisis.'

[Trump straightens his tie with a satisfied nod] 'The calls are coming in nonstop. World leaders, CEOs, everyone wants a piece of this. When you have political capital like this, you don't spend it — you invest it. Bigly.'
resourceGood: [Trump glances at his phone, scrolls briefly, sets it down] 'Numbers are solid. Not the best they've been — could always be better — but solid. Congress is cooperating, the donors are answering calls. We're in a good position to make moves.'

[Trump nods at the briefing folder] 'The political machine is running. Not perfect, but running. McConnell called twice yesterday. When Mitch calls twice, that means the votes are there. Let's use them.'

[Trump leans forward, elbows on the desk] 'We've got enough capital to work with. The base is steady, the Hill is manageable. Not the landslide numbers I want, but enough to push things through. Smart play is to keep building.'
resourceStrained: [Trump's jaw tightens, he flips his phone face-down] 'The polls are softening. I can feel it. The donors are still calling but the conversations are shorter. We need to generate some momentum before the narrative shifts against us.'

[Trump stands abruptly, pacing behind the desk] 'Congress is getting squirrelly. Two senators dodged my calls yesterday — TWO. That never happens when the numbers are strong. We need a win that plays on every channel.'

[Trump stares at the muted TV, arms crossed] 'They smell weakness. The media, the Democrats, even some of our own people. Political capital is a funny thing — once people think you're losing it, you lose it twice as fast. We need to move.'
resourceLow: [Trump sits very still, eyes narrowed, voice quieter than usual] 'The donor calls... they're not returning them as fast. Lindsey's been dodging me. The whip count on the supplemental is soft. We're not in crisis yet but we're one bad headline away.'

[Trump grips the armrest, knuckles white] 'I've been here before. Everyone counts you out, everyone says it's over. But I always come back. Always. We just need one big play — one thing the cameras can't ignore — and the capital flows back.'

[Trump's voice drops, almost conspiratorial] 'Between you and me — and this stays in this room — we're running thin. The Hill is wobbly, the base needs feeding. Don't tell Scavino I said that. Just find me a win.'
resourceCritical: [Trump stares at the desk, uncharacteristically quiet, then slams his palm down] 'They're circling. I can feel it. The 25th Amendment whispers, the impeachment talk — it's not just CNN anymore, it's Fox. When Fox turns, it's real. We need a miracle or we're done.'

[Trump's voice is hoarse, his tie loosened for the first time anyone can remember] 'Forty years in business, eight years in politics, and I've never been this close to the edge. The donors are gone. Congress is writing speeches. One more day like this and they invoke the 25th.'

[Trump pulls out his phone, puts it away, pulls it out again] 'Truth Social is the only platform that still works for me. Every other channel is running the obituary. The political capital is gone — not low, GONE. We need an act of God or an act of genius. Same thing, frankly.'

resourceExcellent: [Hegseth stands at the tactical board, pointer in hand, posture perfect] 'Command authority is absolute. The Joint Chiefs executed every directive without question yesterday. Force readiness is optimal, chain of command is tight. This is what military leadership looks like when it's working.'

[Hegseth sets down a stack of after-action reports with quiet satisfaction] 'Every branch commander reported full compliance this morning. No leaks, no second-guessing, no Pentagon back-channeling to Congress. When your authority is this solid, you can afford to be bold.'

[Hegseth adjusts his cufflinks, calm and measured] 'The brass respects results. We've delivered results. CENTCOM, SOCOM, the carrier group — they're all executing my directives to the letter. This level of institutional trust takes years to build. We built it in weeks.'
resourceGood: [Hegseth reviews the morning SITREP, marking items with a red pen] 'Command structure is functioning well. The Joint Chiefs are aligned on strategy, CENTCOM is responsive. A few grumbles from the old guard about pace of operations, but nothing that affects execution.'

[Hegseth nods at the force disposition map] 'Authority is solid. Not ironclad — there's always friction between the Pentagon and the field — but the important orders are getting through clean. The operators trust the chain of command. That's what matters.'

[Hegseth closes his laptop, folds his hands] 'The brass is cooperating. Not enthusiastically on every front, but cooperating. We have enough institutional trust to run complex operations. Let's not waste it on anything that doesn't move the needle.'
resourceStrained: [Hegseth pauses mid-briefing, jaw set, choosing his words carefully] 'I'm hearing pushback. Not open defiance — not yet — but the Joint Chiefs are asking more questions, requesting more justification. The kind of friction that slows operations when you need speed.'

[Hegseth's hand tightens on his coffee mug] 'Three memos from the Pentagon legal team yesterday. All questioning my operational authority on different grounds. That's not routine — that's coordinated. Someone on the Joint Chiefs is testing how far they can push.'

[Hegseth stares at the tactical board, arms crossed] 'The operators in the field still follow orders. But between me and the field, there are three layers of generals who are starting to add their own interpretations. Command authority means nothing if the chain of command adds footnotes.'
resourceLow: [Hegseth grips the edge of the briefing table, voice tight] 'CENTCOM delayed my last two directives for review. The Joint Chiefs chair requested a private meeting — never a good sign. I've got maybe one more bold operation before they start routing around me.'

[Hegseth is on his feet, pacing the length of the situation room] 'I can feel the brass pulling away. They're not refusing orders — that would be open mutiny — but they're slow-rolling everything. Every directive gets studied to death. If I don't restore confidence fast, I lose the ability to act.'

[Hegseth lowers his voice, leaning in] 'Admiral Richardson called the White House directly yesterday. Bypassed my office entirely. That's not a bureaucratic mistake — that's a signal. I need a clean, visible military success to remind them who's in command.'
resourceCritical: [Hegseth sits alone at the head of the briefing table, the chairs around him conspicuously empty] 'They're not coming to the morning brief. The Joint Chiefs sent a deputy. A DEPUTY. The carrier group commander is taking calls from Congress before returning mine. My authority exists on paper only.'

[Hegseth's hands are flat on the table, his voice barely controlled] 'I got a call from the White House chief of staff this morning asking if I'd consider a graceful exit. A GRACEFUL EXIT. The brass has lost confidence and they've told the Hill. One more fumble and they ask for my stars.'

[Hegseth stands at the window, back to the room, very still] 'Twenty years in uniform. Two combat deployments. I know what a command collapse looks like from the inside. The orders go out and nothing comes back. The phones ring but nobody's on the other end. That's where we are.'

resourceExcellent: [Kushner adjusts his cuffs, a faint smile playing at the corners of his mouth] 'Nobody knows we're here. The back-channels are invisible, the deals are moving, and not a single reporter has connected the dots. This is how real power works — in the spaces between the headlines.'

[Kushner checks his phone — three encrypted messages — and pockets it casually] 'Exposure is minimal. MBS, the Emirati Crown Prince, the Oman back-channel — all of it is running below the radar. The press is chasing yesterday's story while we're building tomorrow's architecture.'

[Kushner leans back, fingers steepled, perfectly composed] 'The beauty of low exposure is optionality. Nobody knows what cards we're holding, so every call we make carries maximum leverage. The Gulf partners trust the discretion. The deals are compounding.'
resourceGood: [Kushner glances at a folded newspaper, pushes it aside] 'Exposure is manageable. A few DC reporters are asking questions about Gulf meetings, but Avi's handling it. The back-channels are intact. We just need to be a little more careful about the paper trail.'

[Kushner scrolls through his tablet, expression neutral] 'Some visibility on the deal pipeline — nothing dangerous, but it means we can't be sloppy. The contacts are still responsive, the Gulf partners are still talking. Just keep the sensitive calls off the official logs.'

[Kushner closes the door before speaking] 'A Politico piece mentioned unnamed senior advisors meeting with Gulf officials. Vague enough to ignore, specific enough to watch. Exposure is in the yellow zone — we operate fine here, but every additional data point is a risk.'
resourceStrained: [Kushner puts his phone in a signal-blocking pouch before speaking] 'We have a visibility problem. Two reporters from the Times are working a back-channel story. They don't have names yet, but they have dates and locations. If they connect the dots, the whole architecture is at risk.'

[Kushner's voice is clipped, all business] 'Exposure is climbing. Someone in the State Department is talking — not much, but enough to create a trail. MBS noticed. He asked Avi directly if the channel was secure. When principals start asking that question, trust erodes fast.'

[Kushner stands at the window, checking the street below — a habit he's developed recently] 'The Washington Post ran a piece on private diplomacy in the Gulf. No names, but the description fits our operation uncomfortably well. I've had to reroute two contact chains already. The infrastructure is getting strained.'
resourceLow: [Kushner enters through the side door, avoiding the main hallway] 'CNN has a source. Not a good source, but they're running with it anyway. My name is in the draft — Avi saw it. We have maybe 48 hours before this becomes a news cycle. Every deal in the pipeline is at risk.'

[Kushner is making calls, his voice barely above a whisper] 'The exposure is critical. MBS has gone dark — not returning calls through the usual channel. The Emirati contact says their principals are nervous about American press attention. Years of relationship-building, and it's unraveling because someone couldn't keep their mouth shut.'

[Kushner sits very still, hands folded, calculating] 'Three reporters have my travel dates. Two Congressional staffers are asking about Gulf meetings. The inspector general's office requested documents. This isn't curiosity anymore — this is a developing investigation. If I don't shut this down, everything collapses.'
resourceCritical: [Kushner stares at his phone — six missed calls from reporters — and sets it face down with deliberate calm] 'It's out. The story broke this morning. Not all of it — not yet — but enough. The back-channels are dead. MBS won't take my calls. The Gulf architecture we spent months building is gone in a news cycle.'

[Kushner's composure cracks for the first time — he loosens his tie, runs a hand through his hair] 'Congressional subpoena came in an hour ago. The press has photos from the Riyadh meeting. The contacts are scattering — every partner we built trust with is now calculating whether knowing me is a liability. This is complete exposure.'

[Kushner is burning papers in the fireplace, phone powered off] 'We're fully exposed. The entire deal pipeline is frozen. Congress is investigating, the press has the timeline, and every Gulf contact has lawyered up. Everything I built — the relationships, the trust, the architecture — it's all coming apart. I need to think.'

resourceExcellent: [Asmongold leans back in his chair, energy drink in hand, grinning at the chat overlay] 'Dude. DUDE. Credibility is through the roof. Every prediction we made? Landed. Chat is going insane. The audience numbers are the highest they've ever been. This is the golden age of content right here.'

[Asmongold pulls up the prediction tracker, every line green] 'Chat, look at this scorecard. We called the tanker routes, we called Iran's move, we called the oil price spike. The analysts on Twitter are quoting US. When the pundits are citing a streamer, you know we're winning.'

[Asmongold slaps the desk with satisfaction] 'OK so here's the thing about credibility — when you have this much of it, people listen to your calls BEFORE they happen. We're not reacting to events anymore, we're SHAPING them. The audience trusts our analysis. That's real power, chat.'
resourceGood: [Asmongold checks the prediction tracker, nods] 'Credibility is solid. Not every call landed, but the hit rate is good enough that the audience trusts the analysis. Chat engagement is healthy. We've got room to make some bold predictions without risking everything.'

[Asmongold takes a sip, scrolls through chat] 'The scorecard looks decent. A few misses, but the big calls were right. The audience isn't questioning the analysis yet — that's the key metric. As long as they're watching and trusting, we have credibility to spend.'

[Asmongold pulls up the analytics dashboard] 'Viewer retention is above average, prediction accuracy is tracking at about 70%. That's not legendary, but it's solid. Chat is engaged, the donations are flowing. We're in a good spot to make some calls.'
resourceStrained: [Asmongold frowns at the prediction tracker, several entries marked red] 'Chat, I'm not gonna sugarcoat it — the last three predictions whiffed. The oil call was wrong, the Iran escalation timing was off. The audience is still here, but you can feel the skepticism creeping into chat. We need a hit.'

[Asmongold rubs his eyes, the chat scrolling with question marks and doubt emojis] 'Credibility is getting shaky. The competing analysts are posting our misses. Twitter is doing the thing where they screenshot your wrong calls. We're not in danger yet, but the trend line is bad.'

[Asmongold leans forward, dead serious for once] 'OK, real talk chat. The prediction accuracy has been mid lately. I can see the viewer count dipping when I make calls now — people are hedging, waiting to see if I'm right before they engage. That's a credibility problem and we need to fix it.'
resourceLow: [Asmongold stares at the analytics, subscriber count trending down] 'Chat... we're in trouble. Credibility is tanking. The last five predictions — three wrong, one inconclusive, one technically right but nobody cared. The audience is leaving. Not a flood, but a steady drain. Every wrong call accelerates it.'

[Asmongold takes off his headset, holds it in his hands, puts it back on] 'I'm going to be honest with you, chat. The analysis has been off. I don't know if it's the data, the methodology, or just bad luck, but the audience doesn't care about excuses. They care about correct calls. And we haven't been making them.'

[Asmongold's voice is quieter, the manic energy gone] 'The competing channels are running highlight reels of my wrong predictions. The donation rate is half what it was a week ago. Chat is memeing on my analysis instead of sharing it. If I don't nail the next big call, this whole thing falls apart.'
resourceCritical: [Asmongold sits in a dark room, monitors casting blue light on his face, chat nearly silent] 'Chat... I don't even know if anyone's still watching. Credibility is gone. Not low — gone. Every prediction I make gets ratio'd instantly. The audience moved to the other analysts. The ones who were quoting me a week ago.'

[Asmongold's voice cracks slightly before he catches himself] 'OK here's where we are. Zero credibility. The prediction tracker is a graveyard. The audience left, the revenue crashed, and the only people still in chat are trolls posting clown emojis. I built this entire operation on being right, and I was wrong. About everything.'

[Asmongold stares at the single-digit viewer count] 'You know the worst part? The analysis might actually be good now. I've learned from the misses. But nobody's listening anymore. Credibility is a one-way door — once you lose it, being right isn't enough. You need to be right AND loud AND lucky. All at once.'

resourceExcellent: [Fuentes slams his fist on the desk, eyes blazing with energy] 'The base is ON FIRE. The rallies are overflowing, the donations are pouring in, Congress is getting flooded with calls. This is what a MOVEMENT looks like. America First isn't a slogan anymore — it's a force of nature.'

[Fuentes holds up his phone — notifications cascading] 'Every America First candidate just got a 10-point bump. The base is mobilized like I've never seen. They're showing up at town halls, they're calling their reps, they're organizing locally. This is the revolution the establishment was afraid of.'

[Fuentes paces with barely contained energy] 'The establishment media is terrified. They're running hit pieces every hour because they know — they KNOW — the base is too strong to stop. When your people believe in something this hard, there's no amount of propaganda that can undo it.'
resourceGood: [Fuentes reviews the rally attendance numbers, satisfied] 'The base is solid. Attendance is strong, online engagement is healthy, the donation pipeline is flowing. Not the peak energy from last month, but steady. The movement is in a good position to push the withdrawal agenda.'

[Fuentes nods at the latest polling] 'America First support is tracking well. The base understands what we're doing and why. Some grumbling about the pace of withdrawal, but they trust the direction. As long as we keep showing progress, they'll stay mobilized.'

[Fuentes closes his laptop, measured confidence] 'Congressional allies report their phones are still ringing with pro-withdrawal calls. The base is engaged and aligned. We have enough grassroots pressure to keep the establishment on their heels. Good position.'
resourceStrained: [Fuentes checks the rally RSVPs, frowns] 'The base is getting restless. Attendance dipped at the last three events. The online forums are starting to ask when we're going to SEE something — not talk about withdrawal, actually DO it. Words without action erode a movement.'

[Fuentes scrolls through comments, jaw tight] 'I'm seeing fractures. The hardcore base is holding, but the broader movement is questioning the timeline. They elected us to bring troops home, not manage a crisis in the Gulf. If we don't show progress soon, the energy bleeds out.'

[Fuentes stares at a map of troop deployments, arms crossed] 'Every day American troops are still deployed in the Gulf is a day the base asks why. The establishment loves a slow walk — it's how they've killed every withdrawal movement for fifty years. Our people see through it. They want action.'
resourceLow: [Fuentes sits in a half-empty rally venue, voice strained] 'The base is slipping. Last rally was half-capacity. The donations dropped 40% this week. The forums are full of people saying we sold out — that we became the establishment the moment we stopped fighting them. I need something to show them.'

[Fuentes grips the podium, alone in the room] 'I'm losing them. The America First base doesn't do patience — they do results. And right now, we have more troops deployed than when we started. Try explaining that at a town hall. Try telling people who believed in you that the timeline shifted.'

[Fuentes turns off his phone after reading the comments] 'The movement is fracturing. Three prominent America First voices broke with us publicly this week. The base follows energy, and right now the energy is turning against us. One big visible withdrawal — even symbolic — might stop the bleeding.'
resourceCritical: [Fuentes stands at an empty podium, the venue echoing, no audience left] 'The base is gone. Not angry — gone. They stopped showing up, stopped calling their reps, stopped donating. A movement without a base is just a guy with a microphone. And the establishment knows exactly what to do with guys who have no base.'

[Fuentes sits in the dark, rally posters peeling off the walls behind him] 'I built America First on a promise — bring the troops home. Simple. Clear. And I failed. The base gave us everything and we gave them nothing. Now they're home, and I'm the one who's lost. Congress is already moving to strip our authority.'

[Fuentes stares at the troop deployment numbers, voice hollow] 'You know what kills a movement? Not opposition — betrayal. Real or perceived, doesn't matter. The base thinks we became the thing we promised to destroy. The rallies are empty. The phones are silent. If we don't do something drastic — I mean TODAY — there's nothing left to save.'

titleScreen.prompt: PRESS ANY KEY OR CLICK TO START

trump: Did you see what CNN just said? They said 'crisis management' like it's a bad thing. Everything I do is crisis management. That's the job.
Hannity texted. He wants an exclusive from the Situation Room. I told him we'd think about it. We should do it.
Oil at $115. You know what that means? It means everyone with a truck in Texas is thinking about me right now. Not in a good way.
The Democrats are already talking impeachment. Impeachment! For defending American interests. These people would impeach Washington for crossing the Delaware.
Get me Hegseth on the phone. No — don't get me Hegseth. He'll want to bomb something. Get me the Fox producer instead.
Polymarket has me at 43% approval tomorrow. I've beaten Polymarket before. Remember 2024?
MBS sent a gift. A gold-plated model of an aircraft carrier. Subtle. Very subtle.
My Truth Social post about the tanker intercept got 4.2 million views. More than CNN's entire primetime combined. Think about that.
The Pentagon wants $2 billion more. For what? Missiles? We've got missiles. I want results, not invoices.
Someone tell the press pool to stop asking about Kushner's travel schedule. It's not relevant. It's a distraction.
hegseth: The 0300 patrol report just came in. Clean sweep. That's three days running. The Navy is doing its job.
I used to be on the other side of that table — the guy getting sent, not the guy sending. I never forget that.
The Joint Chiefs think I'm reckless. Funny. I think they're cowards. Somewhere in between is probably correct.
CNN's running their 'inside the Pentagon' piece again. Every anonymous source in that article works for someone who wants my job.
Air wing sortie rate is at 94%. That's exceptional. Those pilots are the best in the world and they don't get enough credit.
Iron dome intercepted twelve incoming last night. Twelve. Without that system, we'd be counting casualties, not interceptions.
The IRGC commander — Tangsiri — he's good. Not a genius, but competent and brave, which is worse.
I keep this coin in my pocket. 3rd Battalion, Special Forces. Afghanistan, 2012. It reminds me that the people at the pointy end are real.
Someone in this building is leaking. I don't know who, and that's the part that keeps me up at night.
War is the extension of politics by other means. Clausewitz said that. But he didn't have to deal with Twitter.
kushner: MBS just texted an emoji. A handshake emoji. That either means the deal is alive or he's being ironic. With MBS you never know.
My lawyer says the FARA filing isn't technically required because I'm a 'volunteer.' The word 'technically' is doing a lot of work there.
The Omani foreign minister is the most underrated diplomat in the world. He's kept this channel open for six weeks through sheer stubbornness.
Oil at $120 means Affinity Partners' Gulf portfolio is up another $80 million. I'm not supposed to think about that right now.
Araghchi sent a message through the Swiss. Just one word: 'patience.' Either he's playing for time or he's telling me something real.
ProPublica filed another FOIA. My travel logs, my phone records, my hotel bookings. They're building a case. Not legal — narrative.
The Senate Finance Committee wants me to testify. 'Voluntary.' Nothing is ever voluntary in Washington.
Every deal has a price nobody talks about. The Abraham Accords cost the Palestinians. This deal will cost someone too.
The $25 million management fee from PIF hits next month. I am acutely aware of how that looks.
There are three people in this room who know the full deal structure. Everyone else sees what we want them to see.
asmongold: Chat just donated $500 with the message 'glass Iran.' I'm not reading that on stream but it's the top donation of the day.
My OSINT guys in the Discord found something interesting in the shipping AIS data. Might be nothing. Usually is.
Credibility is a weird currency. You spend years building it and one bad prediction destroys it overnight.
The New York Times called me an 'unconventional intelligence asset.' I screenshotted that and it's my new bio.
Someone in the Pentagon is watching my stream. I know because they corrected a ship identification in chat. Username: 'totally_not_an_admiral.'
Reddit is doing that thing where half the posts are genuine analysis and the other half are 'asmongold bald' memes. Par for the course.
My mod team is better than CNN's fact-checking department. That's not a joke, it's a verifiable claim.
Polymarket created a contract on whether my next prediction will be correct. I have never felt more simultaneously flattered and insulted.
Iran's state TV quoted my stream last night. They used a clip where I called the IRGC Navy 'surprisingly competent.' Thanks guys.
I need to eat something. I've been running on Monster Energy and existential dread for fourteen hours.
fuentes: AIPAC spent $12 million on ads against us this week. $12 million. Where does that money come from? Follow the money.
Tucker just texted. 'Stay the course.' That's the most validation I've gotten from anyone outside the base in two weeks.
The European ambassadors won't return my calls. Good. When was the last time a European ambassador solved an American problem?
My Telegram channel hit 500,000 subscribers. Half of them are feds but the other half are real, and that's enough.
The military-industrial complex isn't a conspiracy theory. It's a line item in the federal budget. Look it up.
They call me an isolationist like it's an insult. America First isn't isolation. It's prioritization.
Gas prices are down because we're not burning a trillion dollars in the Gulf. Nobody on CNN will make that connection.
The Secret Service detail keeps getting bigger. Either the threats are real or they're trying to intimidate me. Either way, I'm not stopping.
Netanyahu called. I didn't pick up. He called again. I didn't pick up again. Some messages are better delivered through silence.
They want me to send American kids to die for Saudi oil profits. I'm the radical because I said no.

military.positive: Some ships, some planes — make sure they get the footage. I want Tomahawk launches on the evening news.
military.reluctant: Everything. Send everything. I want Iran to wake up tomorrow and see the entire US Navy on their doorstep. That's how you negotiate.

diplomatic.positive: A deal. I love deals. Nobody makes deals like me. Let's see what they're offering.
diplomatic.reluctant: A summit? Sure, I'll do a summit. I did Singapore with Kim and that was beautiful. This could be bigger.

economic.positive: Sanctions. Easy. Squeeze them until they come to the table. This is what I do best.
economic.reluctant: Crush them. Sanctions on everything — oil, banking, shipping, the works. Make it hurt.

intelligence.positive: Intel. Good. I want to know what they're thinking before they think it.
intelligence.reluctant: Total surveillance. I want to read their emails. I want to know what Tangsiri had for breakfast.

domestic.positive: The American people. That's who matters. This plays great in the heartland — I can feel it.
domestic.reluctant: Congress wants attention again. Give them their briefing. Just make sure they know who's in charge.

military.positive: Solid force package. Two carrier groups gives us strike capability and deterrence. This is how professionals do it.
military.reluctant: Full war footing. I hope you understand what this means — there's no quiet way to undo a deployment this size.

diplomatic.positive: Diplomacy backed by credible force. That's the only kind that works. I can support this.
diplomatic.reluctant: You're betting everything on a handshake with people who've broken every agreement they've ever signed. Your call.

economic.positive: Economic warfare is warfare. I respect it. Just don't expect it to work fast.
economic.reluctant: Heavy sanctions drive desperate regimes to desperate actions. Be ready for Iran to lash out.

intelligence.positive: Solid intel package. HUMINT plus SIGINT gives us the picture we need for targeting.
intelligence.reluctant: Full-spectrum intelligence dominance. We'll know where every fast boat, every missile, every mine is in real time.

domestic.positive: Public support matters for sustained operations. The military can't fight a war the country doesn't believe in.
domestic.reluctant: I'm a military man, not a politician. But even I know you can't run a war without domestic support.

military.positive: Military presence has a diplomatic function. Iran will notice. More importantly, MBS will notice, and that helps the deal.
military.reluctant: You're making diplomacy impossible. Every escalation narrows my options.

diplomatic.positive: This is the architecture I need. Diplomatic capital, backchannel access, Gulf state buy-in. We can build something real.
diplomatic.reluctant: Give me this and I'll get you a deal. A real one. Not a photo op — a framework that reshapes the Middle East.

economic.positive: Targeted sanctions. Useful — but every sanction I add is a card MBS plays against us in the next call.
economic.reluctant: You're cratering their economy. That might sound good in a briefing but desperate economies make desperate governments.

intelligence.positive: The more we know, the better I can negotiate. Information is leverage.
intelligence.reluctant: Careful with cyber operations — if Iran detects the intrusion, the moderate faction loses the argument for diplomacy.

domestic.positive: Managed press exposure is good — but be careful what you tell Congress. Everything leaks.
domestic.reluctant: Domestic politics is noise. The deal gets done in private or it doesn't get done at all.

military.positive: Medium deployment. Two carrier groups means roughly 150 aircraft, 15 surface combatants, and several submarines we can't talk about.
military.reluctant: Full send. We are officially in the 'this could escalate to a shooting war at any moment' zone. Mods, enable slow mode.

diplomatic.positive: Interesting move. The chat is split — hawks are mad, doves are relieved, and the centrists are doing the 'it depends' thing.
diplomatic.reluctant: Full diplomatic commitment. This is either the Lincoln-level play or the Chamberlain-level play. History will decide.

economic.positive: Sanctions analysis incoming. Chat, pull up the OFAC SDN list — I'm going to show you who's actually getting sanctioned.
economic.reluctant: Maximum sanctions. Iran's rial just hit an all-time low. I'll do a stream breaking down the economic data.

intelligence.positive: OSINT plus SIGINT. This is my literal wheelhouse. I'll correlate the classified feeds with open-source data on stream.
intelligence.reluctant: Full intelligence package. If I can cross-reference this with my OSINT network, we'll see things nobody else sees.

domestic.positive: Media blitz? I AM the media blitz. My stream reaches more people than a press conference.
domestic.reluctant: Peak domestic engagement. The audience is the fourth branch of government and they're watching.

military.positive: Fine. Minimal presence. Enough to protect Americans abroad but not enough to get sucked into another forever war.
military.reluctant: Absolutely not. We're not deploying the full US military to protect Saudi oil tankers. The base didn't elect us for this.

diplomatic.positive: If diplomacy means getting out of the Gulf faster, I'll take it. If it means new commitments, forget it.
diplomatic.reluctant: Coalition building? Gulf partnerships? This is the globalist playbook with a different cover. Hard pass.

economic.positive: Sanctions are fine. Cheap, effective, doesn't require American boots on the ground.
economic.reluctant: I'd rather sanction them into the ground than send a single American soldier to die in the Gulf.

intelligence.positive: Intelligence gathering — fine. Knowing what's happening doesn't mean we have to do anything about it.
intelligence.reluctant: I'd rather spend money on intel than on carriers. Information doesn't come home in a body bag.

domestic.positive: Rally the base. Simple. Direct. No intermediaries, no media filter.
domestic.reluctant: Congressional briefing is a trap. They'll use it to build the case for intervention.

asmongold.missile_strike: [Asmongold holds up both hands] 'Dude, no. Chat, he wants to launch missiles. I'm a content creator who got elected on a meme — I am NOT the guy who starts pressing the red buttons. We do intelligence, we do OSINT, we do not do Tomahawk diplomacy.'
asmongold.carrier_strike: [Asmongold spins his chair away from the screen] 'A carrier strike group? Chat, I don't even know what half the buttons on that thing do. You want me to order a floating city to start shooting? That's a skill issue I'm not willing to test. Hard pass.'
asmongold.shock_awe: [Asmongold stares at the camera, dead serious for once] 'OK real talk chat, this is the one card that could literally end the world and also my channel. I got here by being the guy who EXPOSES bad decisions, not the guy who MAKES them. This stays in the deck.'

fuentes.gulf_coalition: [Fuentes slams his palm on the desk] 'A COALITION? With who — the Saudis? The Emirates? Every 'coalition' in history has been America paying the bills while other countries take credit. The whole point of America First is that we stop being the world's ATM.'
fuentes.un_resolution: [Fuentes laughs bitterly] 'A UN resolution. You want to go begging to an organization where Iran has a seat, China has a veto, and America pays 22% of the budget for the privilege of being lectured. The UN is a globalist theater. We don't perform there.'
fuentes.humanitarian_corridor: [Fuentes stands, voice rising] 'Not a single dollar of American taxpayer money leaves this country to fund humanitarian corridors for people who celebrate when Americans die. That's not isolationism — that's common sense. The aid stays home.'
fuentes.summit_proposal: [Fuentes crosses his arms, immovable] 'A summit legitimizes the enemy. You sit down with Iran, you're telling the world they're our equals. The base didn't elect us to shake hands with mullahs. They elected us to bring our people HOME. No summits.'

hegseth.back_channel: [Hegseth sets his jaw, voice flat] 'Back-channel diplomacy. You want me to send someone to whisper in Tehran's ear while our sailors are in harm's way? That's not how warriors solve problems. You negotiate from a position of strength, not from the shadows.'
hegseth.summit_proposal: [Hegseth shakes his head slowly] 'A summit. You want me to sit across a table from the people who seized our ships and smile for cameras. The troops would lose all respect for this office. We meet the enemy on the battlefield, not at a banquet.'

kushner.missile_strike: [Kushner closes the briefing folder with a quiet snap] 'Missile strikes destroy more than targets — they destroy relationships. Every contact I've built in the Gulf goes dark the moment Tomahawks fly. Years of trust, gone in a news cycle. Find another way.'
kushner.active_intercept: [Kushner removes his glasses, polishes them slowly] 'Active intercepts put American vessels in direct confrontation with Iranian boats. The moment CNN has footage of a naval clash, every back-channel in the region shuts down. My contacts don't work with cowboys.'
kushner.shock_awe: [Kushner's voice drops, almost gentle] 'Shock and awe. You know what that looks like from Riyadh? From Abu Dhabi? It looks like America choosing bombs over deals. MBS calls me because I'm the alternative to this. Take this off the table.'

trump.humanitarian_corridor: [Trump waves his hand dismissively] 'Humanitarian corridor? You want me to send aid convoys to people who burn American flags? That's not strength — that's a photo op for the other side. Next.'
trump.war_crimes_tribunal: [Trump leans back, arms crossed, jaw set] 'War crimes tribunal. Let me tell you something about international courts — they're set up to go after US. Not Iran, not China — us. I'm not handing them the ammunition. Absolutely not.'
